title: Egg custard tart
subtitle: Blind-baked shortcrust with a vanilla and nutmeg custard
excerpt: A 23 cm tart with a crisp, buttery shortcrust shell and a set custard filling of eggs, cream, and milk, flavoured with vanilla and a scraping of nutmeg. Eight slices. The custard must be poured into the pre-baked shell while both are warm, or the base will go soggy.
sourceNotes: Egg custard tarts are documented in British cookery from the medieval period. Recipes appear in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), and in pre-1928 British household baking guides. The form is also attested in Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747, Project Gutenberg). The technique of blind-baking the pastry case before filling with custard is documented in pre-1928 patisserie and household baking texts. Cross-referenced with pre-1928 British pastry manuals available through the British Library.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the day before. Keeps refrigerated for 2 days. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 170°C. The custard should be set but with a faint wobble in the centre when the tray is nudged.
baking.decoratingTechnique: grate fresh nutmeg over the filled tart before baking

glossaryTerms.definition: Baking a pastry case without its filling, to set and cook the base before a wet filling is added. The raw pastry is lined with baking paper and weighed down with ceramic baking beans or dried pulses to prevent it puffing up. The beans are removed for the final few minutes to allow the base to dry and colour. Without blind baking, a wet custard filling will soak into the raw pastry, producing a damp, uncooked base. The pastry case must still be warm when the custard is poured in; filling a cold shell creates a temperature differential that can crack the pastry or cause the custard to set unevenly.

content.text: An egg custard tart needs two things done correctly: a
that will not turn soggy, and a custard poured and baked at a low temperature so it sets without curdling. Eight slices. The pastry needs an hour in the fridge before lining the tin, so start ahead.

Rub the cold butter into the flour, icing sugar, and salt until it resembles breadcrumbs. Add the egg yolks and mix; add ice-cold water a teaspoon at a time until the dough just comes together. Flatten into a disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 1 hour.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan (190°C conventional). Roll the chilled pastry to 3 mm and line the tart tin, pressing into the corners. Trim the overhang. Chill for 15 minutes. Line with baking paper, fill with baking beans, and bake for 20 minutes. Remove the paper and beans and bake for a further 8 to 10 minutes until the base is pale golden and dry. Reduce the oven to 160°C fan (170°C conventional).

Whisk the eggs, egg yolks, sugar, and vanilla together in a jug until smooth. Heat the cream and milk in a saucepan until just steaming but not boiling, then pour slowly over the egg mixture, whisking constantly. Skim any foam from the surface. Pour the custard into the still-warm pastry shell in the oven, pulling out the shelf to avoid spilling. Grate the nutmeg generously over the top. Bake at 160°C fan for 25 to 30 minutes, until the edges are set but the centre still wobbles gently when nudged. Cool in the tin for 1 hour, then refrigerate for at least 1 hour before serving.
